aliases: Soft Shell Crab with Salted Egg Sauce
Salted Egg Yolk Soft Shell Crab

english: Soft Shell Crab with Salted Egg Sauce
Salted Egg Yolk Soft Shell Crab

food_subgroup: Crustaceans, battered and fried with egg-based sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for salted egg soft shell crab. FOODON_03302757 (crab food product) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific node for salted egg soft shell crab. A0CJW (Crab, cooked) is the closest defensible parent. Salted egg yolk sauce and batter coating are not captured.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226791003 (Crab) is the closest available concept. The prepared dish with salted egg yolk sauce is not represented as a distinct SNOMED entity.

preparation_method: tossed in salted egg yolk sauce

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of salted egg soft shell crab is approximately 250 g, comprising one or two whole soft shell crabs coated in batter and tossed in salted egg yolk sauce with curry leaves and chilli.

nutrient_source.title: Soft-shell crab, fried — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; salted egg yolk sauce composition estimated from component analysis using USDA Foundation Foods (butter, egg yolk, evaporated milk) and IMR Malaysia MyFCD (salted duck egg)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / IMR Malaysia
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for salted egg soft shell crab as a prepared dish. Nutrient values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for fried soft shell crab (NDB 15148) with a weighted component estimate for salted egg yolk sauce (butter, salted duck egg yolk, evaporated milk, curry leaves, chilli) using USDA Foundation Foods and IMR Malaysia MyFCD. Approximate dish composition assumed: 55% crab with batter, 30% salted egg sauce, 15% aromatics. Values represent a best-estimate per 100 g of the combined dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; analogous estimate based on battered fried seafood and egg-based sauce components
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for salted egg soft shell crab. GI estimated at 45 based on analogous battered fried seafood dishes (GI ~40–50, low category) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. The dish is predominantly protein and fat with moderate batter-derived carbohydrate; the salted egg yolk sauce contributes fat and protein with minimal glycemic carbohydrate, supporting a low GI estimate. Glycemic load per 250 g serving calculated as (45 × 8.4 g carbohydrate × 2.5) / 100 = 9.45, rounded to 9.5.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~620 mg per 100 g) from salted duck egg yolk and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid salted egg soft shell crab due to its very high sodium content (~1550 mg per restaurant serving), which substantially exceeds the recommended 600 mg sodium per meal threshold for blood pressure management.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate from wheat batter with low GI; high saturated fat from salted egg yolk sauce
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low glycemic index (~45) and moderate carbohydrate content make this dish relatively acceptable for glycaemic control, but the high saturated fat from butter and salted egg yolk may contribute to insulin resistance and should be factored into overall dietary fat targets for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High cholesterol (~185 mg per 100 g), high saturated fat, and high sodium from salted egg yolk and butter
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Salted egg soft shell crab is high in dietary cholesterol, saturated fat, and sodium — all established cardiovascular risk factors — and individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish only occasionally and in small portions, ideally within a meal plan reviewed by a dietitian.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Salted Egg Soft Shell Crab is a popular Malaysian and Singaporean seafood dish featuring whole soft shell crabs that are coated in a light wheat flour batter and deep-fried until golden and crispy, then tossed in a rich, aromatic salted duck egg yolk sauce made with butter, evaporated milk, curry leaves, and fresh chilli. The dish delivers a distinctive combination of textures — a crunchy exterior giving way to tender crab flesh — and a deeply savoury, creamy, slightly spicy flavour profile characteristic of the salted egg yolk cooking style that has become widely popular across Southeast Asian cuisines. Nutritionally, the dish is energy-dense at approximately 218 kcal per 100 g, with a significant protein contribution (~14 g per 100 g) from the crab and egg, and a high fat content (~15 g per 100 g) dominated by saturated fat from the butter and salted egg yolk sauce. Sodium content is notably high (~620 mg per 100 g), making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 45 (low), reflecting the protein- and fat-dominant composition with only moderate batter-derived carbohydrate. Clinically, the dish is best consumed occasionally and in controlled portions due to its high sodium, saturated fat, and cholesterol load. It is not suitable for halal diets unless prepared with halal-certified ingredients, and contains major allergens including crustaceans, eggs, gluten, and dairy. The dish is commonly served as a main course in Malaysian tze char restaurants and seafood establishments across Singapore.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for salted egg soft shell crab as a prepared dish. Nutrient values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for fried soft shell crab with a weighted component estimate for the salted egg yolk sauce using USDA Foundation Foods and IMR Malaysia MyFCD. The GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database based on comparable battered fried seafood. Medium confidence reflects the composite estimation approach rather than a direct dish-level measurement.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy fried crab entry or the IMR Malaysia MyFCD salted egg component data used for this estimation. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the primary component sources used. All other -1 values in per_100g have been replaced with estimated numeric values where component data permitted. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol, vitamin C, vitamin A, B-vitamins, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water are estimated from USDA Foundation Foods component data for fried soft shell crab and butter/egg yolk. The dish is not confirmed halal as salted duck eggs and butter sourcing varies by restaurant. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of the accessed date. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (45 × 8.4 × 2.5) / 100 = 9.45, reported as 9.5.
